title: Chocolate digestive biscuits
subtitle: Wholemeal oat biscuits dipped in milk chocolate
excerpt: Homemade digestive biscuits made with wholemeal flour and oats, baked until crisp, then dipped base-down in melted milk chocolate. The biscuit is lightly sweet and wheaty; the chocolate sets hard and snappy. Makes about twenty biscuits.
sourceNotes: The digestive biscuit as a category predates its commercial industrialisation. Wholemeal-and-oat plain biscuits described as digestive appear in Victorian household manuals, where their value was thought to lie in the bran content. No single public-domain recipe documents this precise construction. The chocolate-coated form follows the popular British kitchen version widely reproduced in twentieth-century home-baking guides.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The biscuits keep in an airtight tin for up to five days before the chocolate coating.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 170°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Pricking the surface of raw dough with a fork before baking. This allows steam to escape, preventing the dough from puffing and keeping thin biscuits flat and even. It also helps the biscuit bake through rather than forming a hollow interior.

content.text: The key to a good homemade digestive is the oat-to-flour ratio. Too much oat and the biscuit is crumbly and won't hold when dipped; too little and you lose the wheaty, nutty flavour that makes a digestive what it is. Roll them thin: a fat digestive bakes unevenly and never gets the right snap.

Milk chocolate is classic. Dark chocolate gives more contrast against the sweet dough. Either works. Temper the chocolate properly or just melt it and allow it to set at room temperature — it will be less snappy but still good.

Preheat the oven to 160°C fan. Combine the {{wholemeal-flour}}, {{plain-flour}}, {{rolled-oats}}, {{light-brown-sugar}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} in a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture resembles damp sand. Add the {{whole-milk}} and mix until a firm dough forms. It should hold when pressed.
On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to 3–4 mm thick. Cut into rounds using a 6 cm cutter.

each biscuit all over with a fork. Arrange on a lined baking tray and bake for 14–16 minutes, until firm to the touch and just beginning to darken at the edges. The biscuits will feel slightly soft in the centre; they harden as they cool. Transfer to a rack and cool completely.

Melt the {{milk-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, stirring until smooth. Dip the flat base of each biscuit into the chocolate, let the excess drip off, and set chocolate-side up on a rack or sheet of baking paper. Leave at room temperature until set, about 1 hour.
